name: Mock Test - 05
instruction: The Question Paper contains three sections. Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions. Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any20 questions. Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions. All questions carry equal marks. There is no negative marking.

questions.question_text: Two infinitely long parallel conducting plates having surface charge densities {tex}+\sigma{/tex} and {tex}-\sigma{/tex} respectively are separated by a small distance. The medium between the plates is a vacuum. If {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} is the dielectric permittivity of vacuum, then the electric field in the region between the plates is
questions.question_html: Two infinitely long parallel conducting plates having surface charge densities {tex}+\sigma{/tex} and {tex}-\sigma{/tex} respectively are separated by a small distance. The medium between the plates is a vacuum. If {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} is the dielectric permittivity of vacuum, then the electric field in the region between the plates is
questions.explanation: E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}+\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Vm -1

questions.question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is charged and the charging battery is then disconnected. If the plates of the capacitor are moved farther apart by means of insulating handles _________ .
questions.question_html: A parallel plate capacitor is charged and the charging battery is then disconnected. If the plates of the capacitor are moved farther apart by means of insulating handles _________ .

options.text: the voltage across the plates decreases
options.html: the voltage across the plates decreases

options.text: the electrostatic energy stored in the capacitor increases
options.html: the electrostatic energy stored in the capacitor increases

options.text: the charge on the capacitor increases
options.html: the charge on the capacitor increases

questions.correct_option_text: the electrostatic energy stored in the capacitor increases
questions.explanation: The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor of area A and plate separation d is given by {tex}C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}{/tex} When the plate separation is d 1 where d 1 > d, the capacitance {tex}{C_1} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{{d_1}}}{/tex} Since, {tex}{d_1} > d,{C_1} < C{/tex} The plates are moved apart after disconnecting the battery. The charge Q on the capacitor in both cases remains the same. The initial energy of the capacitor {tex}{U_1} = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{2C}}{/tex} and the final energy after moving the plate 'd' to a distance d 1 , is given by {tex}{U_2} = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{2{C_1}}}{/tex} Since, {tex}{C_1} < C{/tex} therefore, {tex}{U_2} > {U_1}{/tex} Hence, the energy stored in the capacitor increases.

questions.question_text: Inversion temperature of a thermocouple is the temperature of the hot junction at which the emf is:
questions.question_html: Inversion temperature of a thermocouple is the temperature of the hot junction at which the emf is:
questions.explanation: At the temperature of inversion, thermo emf is zero.

questions.question_text: The torque acting on electric dipole of the dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} placed in a uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is
questions.question_html: The torque acting on electric dipole of the dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} placed in a uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is
questions.explanation: Torque on a dipole, {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Power dissipated across the 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor in the circuit shown here is 2 W. The power dissipated in the 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor is:
questions.question_html: Power dissipated across the 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor in the circuit shown here is 2 W. The power dissipated in the 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor is:
questions.explanation: P.D. across (1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} + 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} ) resistors = P.D. across 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor I 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 I 1 = 2 I 2 Power dissipated across 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor I 2 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 2 W or I 2 2 = 0.25 Power dissipated across 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor = I 1 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = (2 I 2 ) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 3 W

questions.question_text: The equivalent resistance of the arrangement of resistances shown in the given figure between points P and Q is:
questions.question_html: The equivalent resistance of the arrangement of resistances shown in the given figure between points P and Q is:
questions.explanation: Equivalent resistance of 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} connected in parallel is given by {tex}\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{16}+\frac{1}{16}=\frac{4}{16}=\frac{1}{4} \therefore R^{\prime}=4 \Omega{/tex} Total resistance in upper arm = 4 + 20 = 24 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Equivalent resistance of 9 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 18 {tex}\Omega{/tex} connected in parallel is {tex}R^{\prime \prime}=\frac{9 \ \times \ 18}{9 \ + \ 18}=6 \Omega{/tex} Total resistance in lower arm = 6 + 6 = 12 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Now 24 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 12 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistances are in parallel. {tex}\therefore{/tex} R PQ = {tex}\frac{24 \ \times \ 12}{24 \ + \ 12}=8 \Omega{/tex}

questions.question_text: A coil of resistance 400 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is placed in a magnetic field. If the magnetic flux {tex}\phi{/tex} (Wb) linked with the coil varies with times t (sec) as {tex}\phi{/tex} = 50t 2 + 4, the current in the coil at t = 2 sec is:
questions.question_html: A coil of resistance 400 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is placed in a magnetic field. If the magnetic flux {tex}\phi{/tex} (Wb) linked with the coil varies with times t (sec) as {tex}\phi{/tex} = 50t2 + 4, the current in the coil at t = 2 sec is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-100 t{/tex} I(at = 2s) {tex}=\frac{|\varepsilon|}{R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{100 \times 2}{400}{/tex} = 0.5 A

questions.question_text: The magnetic susceptibility of an ideal diamagnetic substance is
questions.question_html: The magnetic susceptibility of an ideal diamagnetic substance is
questions.explanation: For a diamagnetic substance, {tex}-1 \leq \chi_{m}<0{/tex} For an ideal or perfect diamagnetic substance, {tex}\chi_m{/tex} = -1 Superconductors are perfectly diamagnetic.

questions.question_text: A dynamo works on the principle of:
questions.question_html: A dynamo works on the principle of:
questions.explanation: Electromagnetic induction, the electric dynamo uses rotating coils of wire and magnetic fields to convert mechanical rotation into a pulsing direct electric current through Faraday's law of induction.

questions.question_text: In an LR-circuit; {tex}L=\frac{0.4}{\pi}{/tex} and R = 30 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . If the circuit has an alternating emf of 220 volt 50 cycles per sec, the impedance and current in the circuit will be
questions.question_html: In an LR-circuit; {tex}L=\frac{0.4}{\pi}{/tex} and R = 30{tex}\Omega{/tex}. If the circuit has an alternating emf of 220 volt 50 cycles per sec, the impedance and current in the circuit will be
questions.explanation: {tex}X_{L}=2 \pi f L=2 \pi \times 50 \times \frac{0.4}{\pi}=40 \Omega{/tex} {tex}Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+X_{L}^{2}}=\sqrt{30^{2}+40^{2}}=50 \Omega{/tex} {tex}I_{\text {eff }}=\frac{V_{\text {eff }}}{Z}=\frac{220}{50}{/tex} = 4.4 A

questions.question_text: The magnetic field at the centre of a current-carrying circular loop having 1 A current and number of turns one will be (radius of the loop is 1 m):
questions.question_html: The magnetic field at the centre of a current-carrying circular loop having 1 A current and number of turns one will be (radius of the loop is 1 m):
questions.explanation: {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{o} N I}{2 a}=\frac{\mu_{e} \times 1 \times 1}{2 \times 1}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: In a metre bridge, the balancing length from the left end (standard resistance of 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is in the right gap) is found to be 20 cm. The value of the unknown resistance is:
questions.question_html: In a metre bridge, the balancing length from the left end (standard resistance of 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is in the right gap) is found to be 20 cm. The value of the unknown resistance is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{R}{S}=\frac{l}{100 \ - \ l_1}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{R}{1 \Omega}=\frac{20 \mathrm{~cm}}{100 \mathrm{~cm} \ - \ 20 \mathrm{~cm}}{/tex} or {tex}R=\frac{20}{80} \times 1 \Omega=0.25 \Omega{/tex}

questions.question_text: Two coils have a mutual inductance 0.005 H. The current changes in the first coil according to equation I = I 0 sin {tex}\Omega t{/tex} , where I 0 = 10 A and {tex}\omega{/tex} = 100 {tex}\pi{/tex} rad s -1 . The maximum value of emf in the second coil is
questions.question_html: Two coils have a mutual inductance 0.005 H. The current changes in the first coil according to equation I = I0 sin {tex}\Omega t{/tex}, where I0 = 10 A and {tex}\omega{/tex} = 100 {tex}\pi{/tex} rad s-1. The maximum value of emf in the second coil is
questions.explanation: {tex}\varepsilon=M \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=M \frac{d}{d t}\left[I_{0} \sin \omega t\right]=M I_{0} \omega \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}\mathcal{E}_{\max }=M I_{0} \omega{/tex} [Max. value of cos {tex}\omega t{/tex} = 1] {tex}=0.005 \times 10 \times 100 \pi=5 \pi \mathrm{V}{/tex}

questions.question_text: In an a.c. circuit, the voltage applied is E = E 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t. The resulting current in the circuit is I = I 0 sin ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t - {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} ). The power consumption in the circuit will be
questions.question_html: In an a.c. circuit, the voltage applied is E = E0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t. The resulting current in the circuit is I = I0 sin ({tex}\omega{/tex}t - {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex}). The power consumption in the circuit will be
questions.explanation: Here, {tex}{\phi} = {\frac \pi 2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Power factor, cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = cos {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} = 0 The power consumed in the circuit, P = E v I v cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = E v I v {tex}\times{/tex} 0 = 0

questions.question_text: A point charge Q is placed at the mid point of a line joining two charges, 4q and q. If the net force on charge q is zero, then Q must be equal to:
questions.question_html: A point charge Q is placed at the mid point of a line joining two charges, 4q and q. If the net force on charge q is zero, then Q must be equal to:
questions.explanation: Force on q due to 4q, {tex}F_1=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_o}\frac{4q^2}{d^2}{/tex} Force on q due to Q, {tex}F_2=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_o}\frac{Qq}{d^2/4}=\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_o}\frac{4Qq}{d^2}{/tex} For equilibrium, F 1 + F 2 = 0 (resultant force is 0) {tex}\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_o}\frac{4Qq}{ d^2}+\frac1{4\pi\varepsilon_o}\frac{4q^2}{d^2}=0{/tex} Hence on solving we get, Q = -q

questions.question_text: The energy stored in a capacitor of capacitance C and potential V is given by:
questions.question_html: The energy stored in a capacitor of capacitance C and potential V is given by:
questions.explanation: {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A current-carrying circular loop of radius R is placed in the x-y plane with centre at the origin. Half of the loop with x > 0 is now bent so that it now lies in the y-z plane.
questions.question_html: A current-carrying circular loop of radius R is placed in the x-y plane with centre at the origin. Half of the loop with x > 0 is now bent so that it now lies in the y-z plane.

options.text: The magnitude of the magnetic moment now diminishes.
options.html: The magnitude of the magnetic moment now diminishes.

options.text: The magnetic moment does not change.
options.html: The magnetic moment does not change.

options.text: The magnitude of B at (0, 0, z), z >>R is unchanged.
options.html: The magnitude of B at (0, 0, z), z >>R is unchanged.

options.text: The magnitude of B at (0, 0, z), z >> R increases.
options.html: The magnitude of B at (0, 0, z), z >> R increases.

questions.correct_option_text: The magnitude of the magnetic moment now diminishes.
questions.explanation: According to Fleming's left hand rule the direction of the magnetic field due to current carrying circular loop is perpendicular and it is perpendicular to the plane of loop and unidirectional. In the first case, the direction of the magnetic field is only in positive x - z-direction but when it is bent then B due to half loop is along -x axis (due to unfolded loop) and B due to other half loop is along +x direction and equal in magnitude so vector sum of equal and opposite B cancel out each other.

questions.question_text: Which quantity is increased in a step-down transformer?
questions.question_html: Which quantity is increased in a step-down transformer?
questions.explanation: Current increases in a step-down transformer.

questions.question_text: Angle of dip is 90° at
questions.question_html: Angle of dip is 90° at
questions.explanation: The angle of dip is 90° at poles.

questions.question_text: Si and Cu are cooled from 300 K to a temperature of 60 K. Then resistivity:
questions.question_html: Si and Cu are cooled from 300 K to a temperature of 60 K. Then resistivity:

options.text: increases for both Si and Cu
options.html: increases for both Si and Cu

options.text: decreases for both Si and Cu
options.html: decreases for both Si and Cu

options.text: for Si increases and for Cu decreases
options.html: for Si increases and for Cu decreases

options.text: for Cu increases and for Si decreases
options.html: for Cu increases and for Si decreases

questions.correct_option_text: for Si increases and for Cu decreases
questions.explanation: Si is a semiconductor, its resistivity increases with the decrease in temperature. Cu is a conductor, its resistivity decreases with the decrease in temperature.

questions.question_text: Gauss's law is valid for
questions.question_html: Gauss's law is valid for
questions.explanation: Gauss's law is valid for any closed surface.

questions.question_text: A circuit containing a 80 mH inductor and a 60 {tex}\mu {\rm{F}}{/tex} capacitor in series is connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz supply. The resistance of the circuit is negligible. Average powers transferred to the inductor and to the capacitor are respectively
questions.question_html: A circuit containing a 80 mH inductor and a 60 {tex}\mu {\rm{F}}{/tex} capacitor in series is connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz supply. The resistance of the circuit is negligible. Average powers transferred to the inductor and to the capacitor are respectively
questions.explanation: {tex}P = VI\cos \phi {/tex} Average power consumed by inductor is zero as actual voltage leads the current by {tex}{\pi \over 2}{/tex} and {tex}\left( {\cos {\pi \over 2} = 0} \right){/tex} . Average power consumed by capacitor is zero as actual voltage lags the current by {tex}{\pi \over 2}{/tex} and {tex}\left( {\cos {\pi \over 2} = 0} \right){/tex} .

questions.question_text: The electric fan works on the principle of :
questions.question_html: The electric fan works on the principle of :
questions.explanation: Electric motor

questions.question_text: If a magnetic material, moves from stronger to weaker parts of a magnetic field, then it is known as
questions.question_html: If a magnetic material, moves from stronger to weaker parts of a magnetic field, then it is known as
questions.explanation: A diamagnetic material moves from stronger to weaker parts of a magnetic field.

questions.question_text: A particle having charge q and mass m starts moving from the origin under the action of an electric field {tex}\vec{E}=E_{0} \hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}=B_{0} \hat{i}{/tex} with a velocity {tex}v=v_{0} \hat{j}{/tex} . The speed of the particle will become {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} v_{0}{/tex} after a time
questions.question_html: A particle having charge q and mass m starts moving from the origin under the action of an electric field {tex}\vec{E}=E_{0} \hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}=B_{0} \hat{i}{/tex} with a velocity {tex}v=v_{0} \hat{j}{/tex} . The speed of the particle will become {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} v_{0}{/tex} after a time
questions.explanation: {tex}v=\sqrt{v_{y}^{2}+v_{x}^{2}}=\sqrt{v_{0}^{2}+\left(\frac{q E}{m} t\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} v_{0}\right)^{2}=v_{0}^{2}+\left(\frac{q E}{m} t\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{m v_{0}}{2 q E}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A coil in the shape of an equilateral triangle of side l is suspended between the pole pieces of a permanent magnet, such that {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} is in plane of the coil. If due to a current I in the triangle, a torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acts on it, the side l of the triangle is:
questions.question_html: A coil in the shape of an equilateral triangle of side l is suspended between the pole pieces of a permanent magnet, such that {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} is in plane of the coil. If due to a current I in the triangle, a torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acts on it, the side l of the triangle is:
questions.explanation: Normal to the plane of the coil will be perpendicular to the field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \tau{/tex} = IBA sin 90 o = IBA Area of an equilateral triangle, {tex}A=\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times l \times l \sin 60^{\circ}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} l^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \tau=I B \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} l^{2} \text { or } l=2\left(\frac{\tau}{\sqrt{3} B I}\right)^{1 / 2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The resistors of resistances 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} are connected in parallel. The total resistance of the combination will be:
questions.question_html: The resistors of resistances 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} are connected in parallel. The total resistance of the combination will be:
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{5}=\frac{19}{20}{/tex} or R = {tex}\frac {20}{19} \Omega{/tex}

questions.question_text: Electric flux is a measure of the
questions.question_html: Electric flux is a measure of the

options.text: area of electric field through a surface
options.html: area of electric field through a surface

options.text: electric field potential through a surface
options.html: electric field potential through a surface

options.text: flow of electric field through a surface
options.html: flow of electric field through a surface

questions.correct_option_text: flow of electric field through a surface
questions.explanation: The number of electric field lines passing through an area normally known as electric flux. The flux is represented by {tex}\overrightarrow E.\widehat n\;ds{/tex} .

questions.question_text: The primary winding of a transformer has 50 turns and its secondary has 500 turns. If the primary is connected to a.c. supply of 20 V-50 Hz, then secondary will have an
questions.question_html: The primary winding of a transformer has 50 turns and its secondary has 500 turns. If the primary is connected to a.c. supply of 20 V-50 Hz, then secondary will have an
questions.explanation: {tex}\varepsilon_{2}=\frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} \cdot \varepsilon_{1}=\frac{500}{50} \times 20=200 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} The frequency remains unchanged.

questions.question_text: When current carrying coil {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{/tex} is moved towards the coil {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{1}}}{/tex} connected in series with a galvanometer, the pointer in the galvanometer G
questions.question_html: When current carrying coil {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{/tex} is moved towards the coil {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{1}}}{/tex} connected in series with a galvanometer, the pointer in the galvanometer G

options.text: shows deflection while {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{/tex} is in motion .

questions.explanation: current induced due to flux changed when C 2 is in motion .

questions.question_text: The work done in turning a magnet of magnetic moment M by an angle of 90° from the meridian is n times the corresponding work done to turn it through an angle of 60°. The value of n is given by:
questions.question_html: The work done in turning a magnet of magnetic moment M by an angle of 90° from the meridian is n times the corresponding work done to turn it through an angle of 60°. The value of n is given by:
questions.explanation: Magnetic moment = M; Initial angle through which magnet is turned {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 90 o Final angle through which magnet is turned {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 60 o Work done in turning the magnet, through90 o W 1 = MB (cos 0 o - cos 90 o ) = MB Similarly, Work done in turning the magnet W 2 = MB(cos 0 o - cos 60 o ) = 0.5 MB Now, W 1 = 2W 2 Given, W 1 = nW 2 n = 2

questions.question_text: The magnetic field between the Horizontal poles of an electromagnet is uniform at any time, but its magnitude is increasing at the rate of 0.020 T/s.The area of a horizontal conducting loop in the magnetic field is 120 {tex}{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} , and the total circuit resistance, including the meter, is {tex}{\rm{5 }}\Omega {/tex} . Induced emf and the induced current in the circuit are
questions.question_html: The magnetic field between the Horizontal poles of an electromagnet is uniform at any time, but its magnitude is increasing at the rate of 0.020 T/s.The area of a horizontal conducting loop in the magnetic field is 120{tex}{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex}, and the total circuit resistance, including the meter, is {tex}{\rm{5 }}\Omega {/tex}. Induced emf and the induced current in the circuit are
questions.explanation: e = Rate of change of magnetic field {tex} \times {/tex} area {tex} = 0.02 \times 120 \times {10^{ - 4}} = 0.24{\rm{mV}}{/tex} {tex}i = {e \over R} = {{0.24} \over 5} = 0.48{\rm{mA}}{/tex}

questions.explanation: The internal resistance of a cell acts only in a closed circuit and it reduces the current.

questions.question_text: Thermo emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} (in volts) of a certain thermocouple is found to vary with {tex}\theta{/tex} (in o C) according to equation E = 20 {tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\frac{\theta^{2}}{20}{/tex} , where {tex}\theta{/tex} is the temperature of the hot function, the cold function being kept at 0° C. Then, the neutral temperature of the thermocouple is:
questions.question_html: Thermo emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} (in volts) of a certain thermocouple is found to vary with {tex}\theta{/tex} (in oC) according to equation E = 20{tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\frac{\theta^{2}}{20}{/tex}, where {tex}\theta{/tex} is the temperature of the hot function, the cold function being kept at 0° C. Then, the neutral temperature of the thermocouple is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\varepsilon=20 \theta-\frac{\theta^{2}}{20} \Rightarrow \frac{d \varepsilon}{d \theta}=20-\frac{\theta}{10}{/tex} At {tex}\theta=\theta_{n}, \ \frac{d \varepsilon}{d \theta}=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore 20-\frac{\theta_{n}}{10}=0 {/tex} or {tex}\theta_{n}=200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 4 cos 1000 t volt is applied to an L-R circuit of inductance 3mH and resistance 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , the amplitude of current in the circuit is
questions.question_html: If emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 4 cos 1000 t volt is applied to an L-R circuit of inductance 3mH and resistance 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, the amplitude of current in the circuit is
questions.explanation: {tex}X_{L}=\omega L=1000 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} \Omega=3 \Omega{/tex} {tex}Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+X_{L}^{2}}=\sqrt{4^{2}+3^{2}}=5 \Omega{/tex} {tex}I_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{Z}=\frac{4}{5}=0.8 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A long solenoid has 800 turns per meter. A current of 1.6 A flows through it. The magnetic induction at the end of solenoid on its axis is
questions.question_html: A long solenoid has 800 turns per meter. A current of 1.6 A flows through it. The magnetic induction at the end of solenoid on its axis is
questions.explanation: The magnetic induction at the end of the solenoid on its axis {tex}B = {1 \over 2}{\mu _0}Ni{/tex} {tex}{\mu _0} = 4\pi \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} N/A 2 N = 800 turns/meter i = 1.6 amp {tex}B = {1 \over 2} \times 4\pi \times {10^{ - 7}} \times 800 \times 1.6 = 8.04\times {10^{ - 4}}{\rm{T}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Magnetic permeability is maximum for
questions.question_html: Magnetic permeability is maximum for
questions.explanation: Magnetic permeability is maximum for ferromagnetic substances.

questions.question_text: A step-up transformer operates on a 230 V line and supplies a current of 2 A. The ratio of primary and secondary windings is 1 : 25. The primary current is
questions.question_html: A step-up transformer operates on a 230 V line and supplies a current of 2 A. The ratio of primary and secondary windings is 1 : 25. The primary current is
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{I_{p}}{I_{s}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore I_{p}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}} \cdot I_{s}=\frac{25}{1} \times 2{/tex} = 50 A

questions.question_text: The element of a heater is rated (P, V). If it is connected across a source of voltage {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} , then the power consumed by it will be
questions.question_html: The element of a heater is rated (P, V). If it is connected across a source of voltage {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex}, then the power consumed by it will be
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{P}{4}{/tex} it is given that the element of a heater is rated (P, V). If it is connected across a source of voltage V' = V/2 . we have to find power consumed by element of the heater. here resistance of element, R = V²/P thus P' = (V/2) 2 /(V 2 /P) P' = P/4

questions.question_text: A piece of copper and another of germanium are cooled from room temperature to 80K. The resistance of
questions.question_html: A piece of copper and another of germanium are cooled from room temperature to 80K. The resistance of

options.text: copper increases and that of germanium decreases
options.html: copper increases and that of germanium decreases

options.text: copper decreases and that of germanium increases
options.html: copper decreases and that of germanium increases

questions.correct_option_text: copper decreases and that of germanium increases
questions.explanation: Copper is a conductor and we know that for conductors, resistance is directly proportional to temperature. Therefore on decreasing temperature resistance also decreases. Whereas, germanium is a semiconductor and for semiconductors, resistance is inversely proportional to temperature. So on decreasing temperature resistance increases.

questions.question_text: Correct expression for force on a current carrying conductor of length dl in a magnetic field is
questions.question_html: Correct expression for force on a current carrying conductor of length dl in a magnetic field is
questions.explanation: Force experienced by a conductor of length dl carrying current I placed in magnetic field B is given by {tex}I(\overrightarrow{dl}\;\times\;\overrightarrow B){/tex} .

questions.question_text: A closely wound solenoid of 2000 turns and area of cross-section {tex}{\rm{1}}.{\rm{6 }} \times {\rm{ 1}}{0^{-{\rm{4}}}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} , carrying a current of 4.0 A, is suspended through its centre allowing it to turn in a horizontal plane. What is the magnetic moment associated with the solenoid?
questions.question_html: A closely wound solenoid of 2000 turns and area of cross-section {tex}{\rm{1}}.{\rm{6 }} \times {\rm{ 1}}{0^{-{\rm{4}}}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex}, carrying a current of 4.0 A, is suspended through its centre allowing it to turn in a horizontal plane. What is the magnetic moment associated with the solenoid?
questions.explanation: m = NIA {tex}=2000\times 1.6\times 10^{-4}\times 4{/tex} = 1.28 Am 2

options.html: A is false but R is true.

questions.correct_option_text: A is false but R is true.
questions.explanation: The temperature inside the earth is so high that it is impossible for iron core to behave as a magnet and act as a source of magnetic field. The magnetic field of earth is considered to be due to circulating electric current in the iron (in molten state) and other conducting materials inside the earth. This theory is still at the frontiers of science and must be considered with some reservation.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): Average value of ac over a complete cycle is always zero. Reason (R): Average value of ac is always defined over half cycle.

Assertion (A): Average value of ac over a complete cycle is always zero.
Reason (R): Average value of ac is always defined over half cycle.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

questions.correct_option_text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation: The mean average value of alternating current (or emf) during a half, cycle is given by I m = 0.636 I 0 (or E m = 0.636 E 0 ) During the next half cycle, the mean value of ac will be equal in magnitude but opposite in direction. For this reason the average value of ac over a complete cycle is always zero. So the average value is always defined over a half cycle of ac.

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 52 to 55: For the various charge systems, we represent equipotential surfaces by curves and line of force by full line curves. Between any two adjacent equipotential surfaces, we assume a constant potential difference the equipotential surfaces of a single point charge are concentric spherical shells with their centres at the point charge. As the lines of force point radially outwards, so they are perpendicular to the equipotential surfaces at all points. Identify the wrong statement.
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

For the various charge systems, we represent equipotential surfaces by curves and line of force by full line curves. Between any two adjacent equipotential surfaces, we assume a constant potential difference the equipotential surfaces of a single point charge are concentric spherical shells with their centres at the point charge. As the lines of force point radially outwards, so they are perpendicular to the equipotential surfaces at all points.


Identify the wrong statement.

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 52 to 55: For the various charge systems, we represent equipotential surfaces by curves and line of force by full line curves. Between any two adjacent equipotential surfaces, we assume a constant potential difference the equipotential surfaces of a single point charge are concentric spherical shells with their centres at the point charge. As the lines of force point radially outwards, so they are perpendicular to the equipotential surfaces at all points. The work done in carrying a charge q once round a circle of radius a with a charge Q at its centre is
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

For the various charge systems, we represent equipotential surfaces by curves and line of force by full line curves. Between any two adjacent equipotential surfaces, we assume a constant potential difference the equipotential surfaces of a single point charge are concentric spherical shells with their centres at the point charge. As the lines of force point radially outwards, so they are perpendicular to the equipotential surfaces at all points.


The work done in carrying a charge q once round a circle of radius a with a charge Q at its centre is
questions.explanation: The electrical potential at any point on circle of radius a due to charge Q at its centre is {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Q}{a}{/tex} It is an equipotential surface. Hence, work done in carrying a charge q round the circle is zero.
